It's used for cleaning your glasses.
Sorry, I don't know the word, let me describe it.
Good afternoon! I'm Tomas. How can I help you today?
Good afternoon. I need three things, but, I don't know the words. Let me describe them. The first is a small gadget, made of plastic, used for putting my Brazilian charger into your walls.
Ah, an adapter. The holes are different here, yes. Here you are. And the second thing?
The second is a little tool, made of metal, used for opening a bottle of wine. It's a kind of spiral that goes into the cork.
A corkscrew. Perfect description, I understood right away. And the last one?
The last is a kind of small sticker for your skin. It's similar to a little bandage, you put it on a small cut on your finger.
Plasters. A whole box, here. You know, you described three things you couldn't name, and you got all three. Most people just give up.
For years, when I lost a word, my son finished the sentence for me. This time, I wanted to describe it myself.
It's a kind of small tool, used for opening bottles.
It's similar to a spoon, but smaller.

My name is Marco, and I have worked in a small shop in Lisbon for twenty years, so let me tell you a secret about tourists. Every day, someone comes to my counter, and they need something, and they do not know the word in English or in Portuguese. And I watch the fear arrive on their face. Some of them turn red, they say sorry, sorry, and they walk out with empty hands, and they go home without the thing they came for. And it breaks my heart, because they did not need the word. They needed twenty more seconds and a little courage. Here is what the clever ones do. They do not run. They say: it is a small thing, it is made of metal, and it is used for opening a bottle of wine. And of course, in one second, I know: a corkscrew. Or they say: it is a kind of sticker, it is used for a small cut on your finger. A plaster. Done. I do not need you to know the word. I need you to describe the thing. Tell me what it is made of. Tell me what it is used for. Tell me what it is similar to, or the opposite of. Is it round, is it flat, is it big, is it small? Point at it, draw it in the air with your hands, compare it to something I know. I have been doing this for twenty years, in four languages, and I promise you: nobody has ever left my shop because their grammar was wrong. They leave because they gave up too soon. So do not give up. The word is not the point. The thing is the point. Describe it, and it is yours.

I am Dulce, I am sixty-nine, and for most of my life, one forgotten word could close my mouth for the rest of the day. I would be in the middle of a sentence, in a shop or at a dinner, and the word would simply go, like a light switching off, and I would stop. I would look down at my hands, and I would feel small and stupid, and I would say nothing, and the moment would pass, and somebody else would speak for me. For years I believed that talking meant knowing every word. And then a young teacher told me something that changed my trips completely. She said: Dulce, you already know two thousand words. You will always be missing the exact one you want, in every language, even in Portuguese. That is normal. Even people born speaking English forget words and go around them. The trick is not to have every word. The trick is to never stop. If you lose the word for umbrella, you say: the thing you open when it rains. If you lose the word for pharmacy, you say: the place where you buy medicine. You walk around the missing word, like walking around a hole in the road, and you keep going, and the other person follows you every time. So now, when a word disappears, I do not go quiet anymore. I describe. It is a kind of, it is used for, it is made of, it is similar to. I talk my way around it, and I arrive. And I have never once, in five years, been left standing in a shop with my mouth closed, waiting for a word that was not going to come. Describe it, and keep walking. The word will catch up with you later, and it does not matter if it never does.
